cover.photoAlt: A long dinner table set with candles before guests arrived.

noteFromTheRoom: The conversation ended close to midnight.
This is what could be carried out of the room
without breaking what was said inside it.

signals.title: THE PRICING QUESTION KEPT RETURNING.
signals.body: Several founders returned to the same pricing question: how to move beyond seat-based models when customers increasingly expect outcomes, not access. The conversation moved past frameworks. It became practical — and uneasy.

signals.title: OPERATIONAL AI HAS A QUIET ADOPTION CURVE.
signals.body: The room agreed: the loud half of the AI conversation has moved past most operators. What remains is unsexy integration work, hiring decisions that look mundane on paper, and slow internal trust-building. Several described this as the actual work of 2026.

signals.title: SEVERANCE IS NOW A BRAND EVENT.
signals.body: A pattern emerged in how the room described senior departures: post-LinkedIn, every meaningful exit becomes a 90-day public moment. The discussion turned to severance design, framing language, and the cost of getting it wrong.

briefing.title: A more complete record.
briefing.body: The full briefing extends what's above. Written in the same spirit. Sent only to those who were in the room. Six pages.

voices.quote: The most useful conversations I've had this year happened in rooms exactly this size.
I came expecting a panel. I got a conversation. That's the difference.
You can feel when a room is curated. This one was.

shareCaption: A small dinner with founders and operators in Austin. Some of the most useful conversations of the year happen in rooms like this. Grateful to GILD and Tecla for hosting.
